meta.title: Chapter 5: The Uncertainty Principle — Wolff, Lectures on Harmonic Analysis

sections.body:
Assume that $f \in L^2$ and $\hat{f}$ is supported in $D(0, R)$. Then $f$ is $C^\infty$ and there is an estimate

$$\|D^{\alpha}f\|_{2}\leq(2\pi R)^{|\alpha|}\|f\|_{2}. \tag{55}$$

Essentially this is an immediate consequence of the Plancherel theorem. The Fourier inversion formula

$$f(x)=\int\hat{f}(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\cdot\xi}d\xi \tag{56}$$

is valid (in the naive sense). The support assumption implies $\hat{f} \in L^1$. Choose a sequence of Schwartz functions $\psi_k$ which converges to $\hat{f}$ both in $L^1$ and in $L^2$. Let $\phi_k \in \mathcal{S}$ satisfy $\widehat{\phi}_k = \psi_k$. The formula (56) is valid for $\phi_k$. As $k \to \infty$, the left sides converge in $L^2$ to $f$ by Theorem 3.10 and the right sides converge uniformly to $\int \hat{f}(\xi) e^{2\pi i x \cdot \xi} d\xi$, which proves (56) for $f$.

Proposition 1.3 applied to $\hat{f}$ now implies that $f$ is $C^{\infty}$ and that $D^{\alpha}f$ is obtained by differentiation under the integral sign in (56). The estimate (55) holds since

$$\|D^{\alpha}f\|_{2}=\|\widehat{D^{\alpha}f}\|_{2}=\|(2\pi i\xi)^{\alpha}\hat{f}\|_{2}\leq(2\pi R)^{|\alpha|}\|\hat{f}\|_{2}=(2\pi R)^{|\alpha|}\|f\|_{2}.$$

There is a fixed Schwartz function $\phi$ such that if $f \in L^{1} + L^{2}$ and $\widehat{f}$ is supported in $D(0, R)$, then

$$f=\phi^{R^{-1}}*f.$$

Take $\phi \in \mathcal{S}$ so that $\widehat{\phi}$ is equal to 1 on $D(0,1)$. Thus $\widehat{\phi^{R^{-1}}}(\xi) = \widehat{\phi}(R^{-1}\xi)$ is equal to 1 on $D(0,R)$, so $\widehat{(\phi^{R^{-1}} * f - f)}$ vanishes identically. Hence $\phi^{R^{-1}} * f = f$.

Suppose that $f \in L^{1} + L^{2}$ and $\hat{f}$ is supported in $D(0, R)$. Then:

1. For any $\alpha$ and $p \in [1, \infty]$,

$$\|D^{\alpha}f\|_{p}\leq(C R)^{|\alpha|}\|f\|_{p}.$$

2. For any $1 \leq p \leq q \leq \infty$,

$$\|f\|_{q}\leq C R^{n(\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{q})}\|f\|_{p}.$$

The function $\psi = \phi^{R^{-1}}$ satisfies

$$\|\psi\|_{r}=C R^{\frac{n}{r^{\prime}}} \tag{57}$$

for any $r \in [1, \infty]$, where $C = \|\phi\|_{r}$. Also, using the chain rule

$$\left\|\nabla\psi\right\|_{1}=R\|\phi\|_{1}. \tag{58}$$

We know that $f = \psi * f$. In the case of first derivatives, part 1 therefore follows from (57) and (24). The general case of 1. then follows by induction.

For part 2, let $r$ satisfy $\frac{1}{q} = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{r'}$. Apply Young's inequality:

$$\begin{align*}
\|f\|_{q} &= \|\psi*f\|_{q} \\
&\leq \|\psi\|_{r}\|f\|_{p} \\
&\lesssim R^{\frac{n}{r^{\prime}}}\|f\|_{p} \\
&= R^{n(\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{q})}\|f\|_{p}.
\end{align*}$$

Suppose that $f \in L^{1} + L^{2}$ and $\hat{f}$ is supported in an ellipsoid $E$. Then

$$\|f\|_{q}\lesssim|E|^{(\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{q})}\|f\|_{p}$$

if $1 \leq p \leq q \leq \infty$.

Let $k$ be the center of $E$. Let $T$ be a linear map taking the unit ball onto $E-k$. Let $S=T^{-t}$ (thus $T=S^{-t}$). Let $f_{1}(x)=e^{-2\pi ik\cdot x}f(x)$ and $g=f_{1}\circ S$, so that

$$\begin{array}{rcl}
\hat{g}(\xi) &=& |\det S|^{-1}\widehat{f_{1}}(S^{-t}(\xi)) \\
&=& |\det S|^{-1}\hat{f}(S^{-t}(\xi+k)) \\
&=& |\det T|\hat{f}(T(\xi)+k).
\end{array}$$

Thus $\hat{g}$ is supported in the unit ball, so by Proposition 5.3

$$\|g\|_{q}\lesssim\|g\|_{p}.$$

On the other hand,

$$\|g\|_{q}=|\det S|^{-\frac{1}{q}}\|f\|_{q}=|\det T|^{\frac{1}{q}}\|f\|_{q}=|E|^{\frac{1}{q}}\|f\|_{q}$$

and likewise with $q$ replaced by $p$. So

$$|E|^{\frac{1}{q}}\|f\|_{q}\lesssim|E|^{\frac{1}{p}}\|f\|_{p}$$

as claimed.

Suppose that $f \in L^1 + L^2$ and $\hat{f}$ is supported in an ellipsoid $E$. Then for any dual ellipsoid $E^*$ and any $z \in E^*$,

$$|f(z)|\leq C_{N}\frac{1}{|E^{*}|}\int|f(x)|\phi_{E^{*}}(x)dx. \tag{60}$$

Step 1. Assume $E$ is the unit ball and $E^{*}$ is also the unit ball. Then $f = \psi * f$ with a fixed Schwartz function $\psi$:

$$\begin{align*}
|f(z)| &\leq \int|f(x)|\left|\psi(z-x)\right|dx \\
&\leq C_{N}\int|f(x)|(1+|z-x|^{2})^{-N} \\
&\leq C_{N}\int|f(x)|(1+|x|^{2})^{-N}.
\end{align*}$$

The last step uses $1+|z-x|^2 \gtrsim 1+|x|^2$ uniformly when $|z|\leq 1$. This proves (60) for $E=E^{*}=$ unit ball.

Step 2. Suppose $E$ is centered at zero but $E$ and $E^{*}$ are otherwise arbitrary. Let $k$ and $T$ be as above and consider

$$g(x)=f(T^{-1}x+k).$$

Its Fourier transform is supported on $T^{-1}E$, and $T^{-1}$ maps $E$ onto the unit ball. Accordingly,

$$|g(y)|\leq\int\phi(x)|g(x)|dx$$

if $y \in D(0,1)$, so that

$$f(T^{-1}z+k)\leq\int\phi(x)|f(T^{-1}x+k)|dx=|\det T|\int\phi_{E^{*}}(x)|f(x)|dx$$

by changing variables. Since $|\det T| = \frac{1}{|E^*|}$, we get (60).

Step 3. If $E$ isn't centered at zero, replace $f$ by $e^{-2\pi ik\cdot x}f(x)$ where $k$ is the center of $E$.
